tldr: Headlines collectively frame the Widdecombe killing as a police-procedural story — suspect arrested, attack confirmed as targeted — while Breitbart's two titles inject a culture-war register visible nowhere else, advertising the death as a pretext for left-wing censorship and charging that anti-Farage rhetoric constitutes 'stochastic terrorism.' The headline layer telegraphs a sharper partisan split than the body-text pass.

evidence.quote: killing of former politician Ann Widdecombe

frames.sub_frame: online hate laws and censorship debate

silences.note: ARY News headline ('12-year-old madrasa student, ‘raped’, killed') covers an unrelated domestic Pakistani crime with no connection to the Widdecombe story — swept into the cluster by shared crime vocabulary.
Both Telegraph titles are 2024 archive crime stories (Hessle stabbing; Harshita Brella murder) with no editorial engagement with Widdecombe; UK bucket contributes zero on-story headline framing.

bottom_line: At the headline layer the dominant message is procedural — a former MP killed in a targeted attack, suspect arrested — but Breitbart's two titles are the loudest signal in the corpus, advertising the killing as evidence of left-wing bad faith and censorship ambition rather than as a crime.
